content.text: ADR Review, Approval and Deprecation Lifecycle

An Architecture Decision Record that is written but never reviewed becomes a historical artifact rather than a living policy document. The value of an ADR index lies in its accuracy: the team must be able to look up any architectural decision and trust that what they find reflects the current state of the system, not the state it was in eighteen months ago when the ADR was first written.

Achieving that accuracy requires a defined lifecycle: explicit states that an ADR can occupy, defined triggers that move an ADR from one state to another, a review cadence that keeps accepted ADRs current, and a deprecation process that retires decisions cleanly when the architecture evolves past them. This document describes that lifecycle and the practices that make it work for Claude integrations.

attrs.alt: ADR lifecycle state machine showing Draft, In Review, Accepted, Superseded, Deprecated and Rejected states with transition triggers and review cadence

content.text: ADR States
Every ADR in the index occupies exactly one of the following states at any point in time:
State
Meaning
Who Can Transition In
Who Can Transition Out
DRAFT
Being written; not yet submitted for review
Author (creation)
Author (submit for review)
IN REVIEW
Circulated to stakeholders; open for comment
Author (submit)
Architecture lead (approve or reject); Author (rework → DRAFT)
ACCEPTED
Active policy; the system follows this decision
Architecture lead (approve)
Architecture lead (supersede or deprecate)
REJECTED
Reviewed and not adopted; decision preserved for record
Architecture lead (reject during review)
Terminal state — not re-opened; a new DRAFT is written instead
SUPERSEDED
Previously accepted; replaced by a newer ADR that changed the decision
Architecture lead (when accepting a replacement)
Terminal state — preserved for historical reference
DEPRECATED
Previously accepted; the concern is no longer relevant (feature removed, migration complete, pattern retired)
Architecture lead (explicit deprecation)
Terminal state — preserved for historical reference

REJECTED, SUPERSEDED, and DEPRECATED are terminal states. An ADR that reaches any of these states is never re-activated — if a previously rejected approach later becomes viable, a new DRAFT is written rather than reactivating the old ADR. This rule preserves the historical record: the original ADR and the reasoning for its rejection remain findable.
The Approval Process
What Requires Approval
Not every change to a Claude integration requires an ADR and the approval process. The approval process is reserved for decisions that:
Commit the architecture to a direction that is costly or difficult to reverse (model selection, data flow pattern, authentication architecture)
Establish a policy that affects multiple teams or future development (system prompt governance, cost allocation model, content filtering policy)
Record a security-relevant decision (credential management approach, data handling boundaries, audit logging scope)
Document a significant trade-off where the rejected alternative was seriously considered (the reasoning for the choice matters as much as the choice itself)

Incremental implementation decisions — which library to use within an already-decided architectural pattern, how to structure an internal module, what variable names to use — do not require ADRs. The signal is whether a future engineer reading the ADR would learn something important that is not evident from the code.
Stakeholder Selection

An ADR in review is circulated to a defined set of stakeholders, not sent to a broad mailing list. Broad distribution slows approval and dilutes accountability. The default stakeholder set for a Claude integration ADR is:
Architecture lead (required approver):
The person with architectural authority over the integration. Cannot be the same person as the ADR author for high-impact ADRs.
Operations lead (required reviewer):
Responsible for operating the system in production. Reviews ADRs for operational feasibility and runbook implications.
Security lead (required for security-relevant ADRs):
Reviews authentication, data handling, and content policy ADRs.
Product owner (informed, not approval-required):
Notified of accepted ADRs that affect product behaviour; can raise concerns but does not block approval.
The review period is defined per ADR category:
ADR Category
Review Period
Approval Quorum
Model selection / routing
5 business days
Architecture lead + Operations lead
Security / data handling
5 business days
Architecture lead + Security lead
System prompt governance
3 business days
Architecture lead
Cost / capacity
3 business days
Architecture lead + Operations lead
Operational model
3 business days
Architecture lead + Operations lead
Approval Record
An approval is recorded in the ADR itself, not in a separate approval workflow system. The Status field of the ADR is updated to ACCEPTED, and the approval record is appended:

Status: Accepted
Accepted: 2024-10-15
Approvers:
  - J. Chen (Architecture lead) — approved 2024-10-14
  - M. Okonkwo (Operations lead) — approved 2024-10-15
Review comments resolved: yes
Open items at acceptance: none
Next scheduled review: 2025-01-15

Approval via a pull request approval mechanism (GitHub PR review) is acceptable and provides an audit trail. The pull request must merge the ADR file with the Status field already updated to ACCEPTED — merging a DRAFT ADR is not the same as approving it.
The 90-Day Scheduled Review
Every ACCEPTED ADR has a scheduled review date, set to 90 days from the acceptance date. The 90-day review is not a full re-approval — it is a fitness check that answers four questions:
Has anything changed in the model landscape (new model releases, price changes, capability announcements) that makes the decision sub-optimal?
Has anything changed in the integration itself (usage volume, query distribution, user population) that invalidates the assumptions the ADR was written against?
Has the decision been implemented as written? If the system diverged from the ADR, either the ADR must be updated to reflect reality or the implementation must be corrected.
Are the consequences listed in the ADR playing out as expected? If a predicted negative consequence materialised worse than expected, a revision may be warranted.
The review produces one of three outcomes:
Re-accepted:
The ADR is accurate, the decision remains sound, the review date is advanced by another 90 days.
Amended:
The ADR is updated with revised thresholds, corrected implementation notes, or updated consequences. The updated version goes through a short review cycle (3 business days) before re-acceptance.
Superseded:
A meaningful change to the decision is required. A new DRAFT ADR is written to replace this one. The existing ADR's status becomes SUPERSEDED when the new ADR is accepted.

The 90-day review is the most commonly skipped ADR maintenance activity. Teams that treat it as optional consistently find that their ADR index drifts from reality — the code says one thing, the ADR says another. Scheduling the review as a calendar event at acceptance time, with the architecture lead as the required attendee, is the single most effective practice for keeping ADRs accurate.
Out-of-Cycle Reviews

Certain events trigger an ADR review outside the 90-day schedule. These out-of-cycle reviews are reactive — they are initiated by a specific change in the environment and must be completed within a defined window.
Trigger
Review Window
ADRs to Review
Outcome If Decision Changes
New Claude model release (major version)
14 days from release date
All model-selection and routing ADRs
New DRAFT ADR for revised selection; existing ADR moves to SUPERSEDED
Claude API pricing change
30 days from effective date
All cost/capacity and routing ADRs
Amendment if thresholds change; SUPERSEDED if tier structure changes
Production incident attributed to architectural decision
5 days from incident resolution
ADR(s) implicated in the incident post-mortem
Amendment or SUPERSEDED depending on severity of decision change
Quality regression below guard-rail threshold
14 days from detection
ADRs governing model selection, system prompt policy, or knowledge base design
Amendment if parameter changes resolve it; SUPERSEDED if architectural change required
Security audit finding
5 days from audit report
ADRs in the security / data handling category
Amendment or SUPERSEDED; security findings that require immediate mitigation may precede the formal ADR update
The Supersession Process

Supersession is the formal mechanism for replacing one accepted architectural decision with another. It is not the same as amending an ADR — an amendment refines the existing decision within the same architectural approach, while supersession replaces the approach entirely.
Writing the Superseding ADR
The superseding ADR must:
Reference the superseded ADR explicitly:
Use the field "Supersedes: ADR-XXX" in the header of the new ADR.
Explain why the prior decision is no longer sufficient:

This is the most important section of the superseding ADR. The context section must explain what changed — in the model landscape, in the integration, in business requirements — that makes the existing decision inadequate. A superseding ADR that does not explain why the previous decision was correct at the time and what changed is not a useful historical record.
Describe what was learned from the prior decision:

If the prior ADR's predicted consequences turned out to be accurate or inaccurate, document that. If the implementation of the prior decision revealed unexpected constraints, document those too. This institutional learning is the compounding value of maintaining an ADR index over time.

ADR-012: Model Selection — Contract Review (Revised)
Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-01-20
Supersedes: ADR-007
Reason for supersession: ADR-007 selected claude-sonnet-4-6 as the primary model
  for contract review. Since ADR-007 was written, two changes make this decision
  sub-optimal:
    1. The 90-day review (2024-12-15) found that 34% of contract reviews are
       exceeding the max_tokens budget (ADR-007 set 2048); longer contracts
       are being truncated. This is a systematic quality problem.
    2. The October 2024 Anthropic pricing update reduced Opus token costs by 40%.
       At the revised price point, Opus is within 12% of Sonnet's cost for the
       same contract review task, which is within the acceptable range given
       the quality difference.

WHAT WAS LEARNED FROM ADR-007
  - Token budget was set too conservatively. The 90th percentile contract size
    requires 2,800 tokens, not 2,048.
  - The quality difference between Sonnet and Opus on critical-clause extraction
    was measured at 8% recall improvement (82% → 90%) in the 90-day eval run.
    At the original Opus price, this was not cost-justified; at the revised price
    it is.
  - The routing classifier from ADR-009 has been performing accurately:
    97% of standard-complexity contracts were correctly classified and none
    required escalation. The Opus decision in this ADR applies to contracts
    classified as Tier 3 (complex) only.

DECISION
[revised decision text]
Updating the Superseded ADR
When the superseding ADR is accepted, the superseded ADR's status field is updated to reflect the supersession:

Status: Superseded
Superseded by: ADR-012
Superseded date: 2025-01-20
Note: This ADR was correct for the period 2024-09-01 to 2025-01-20.
      See ADR-012 for the reasoning that led to the supersession.

The superseded ADR remains in the ADR index. It is not deleted or archived. Future engineers reading the index should be able to trace the full decision history: what was decided, what was subsequently replaced, and why.
The Deprecation Process

Deprecation is distinct from supersession. A superseded ADR was replaced by a different decision on the same concern. A deprecated ADR covers a concern that no longer exists — the feature was removed, the migration completed, the integration retired.
Deprecation is initiated by the architecture lead when:
The system component that the ADR governed has been decommissioned
A migration completed and the migration-specific architectural decisions are no longer relevant
The integration entered a maintenance-only state and no new architectural decisions will be made
The ADR covers a pattern that has been replaced everywhere it was applied (all instances migrated, no remaining instances of the old pattern)

Status: Deprecated
Deprecated: 2025-06-01
Reason: The legacy document ingestion pipeline described in this ADR was fully
  replaced by the streaming ingestion service (ADR-031) as of 2025-05-31.
  No instances of the batch ingestion pattern remain in production.
  This ADR is preserved for reference; no operational decisions should
  reference it for current systems.
ADR Index Maintenance

The ADR index is a register of all ADRs — their IDs, titles, statuses, acceptance dates, and next review dates. The index must be kept current with the ADR lifecycle — an out-of-date index is worse than no index because it creates a false sense of completeness.

# docs/adr/index.md — ADR Index
# Updated: 2025-01-20 | Next index review: 2025-04-20

| ID      | Title                                     | Status      | Accepted   | Next Review |
|---------|-------------------------------------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| ADR-001 | System Prompt Governance Policy           | Accepted    | 2024-08-01 | 2025-04-01  |
| ADR-002 | Authentication Architecture               | Accepted    | 2024-08-05 | 2025-05-05  |
| ADR-003 | Knowledge Base Design — Initial           | Superseded  | 2024-08-10 | —           |
| ADR-004 | Data Handling and Retention               | Accepted    | 2024-08-15 | 2025-02-15  |
| ADR-005 | Cost Allocation Model                     | Accepted    | 2024-09-01 | 2025-06-01  |
| ADR-006 | Batch Ingestion Pipeline                  | Deprecated  | 2024-09-05 | —           |
| ADR-007 | Model Selection — Contract Review         | Superseded  | 2024-09-10 | —           |
| ADR-008 | Content Filter Audit Policy               | Accepted    | 2024-09-15 | 2025-03-15  |
| ADR-009 | Request Routing Policy                    | Accepted    | 2024-09-20 | 2025-03-20  |
| ADR-010 | Fallback and Retry Policy                 | Accepted    | 2024-09-20 | 2025-03-20  |
| ADR-011 | Knowledge Base Design — v2                | Accepted    | 2024-11-01 | 2025-08-01  |
| ADR-012 | Model Selection — Contract Review (Rev.)  | Accepted    | 2025-01-20 | 2025-10-20  |
The index itself has a review date — typically the date of the oldest active ADR's next review. This ensures the index is checked at least as frequently as the most urgent ADR review.
Common Lifecycle Failure Modes
Failure Mode
How It Manifests
Prevention
Draft never submitted
ADR document exists in a docs/ folder with Status: Draft for months; the decision was made but not approved
Architecture lead checks for stale DRAFTs at each weekly architecture sync; DRAFTs older than 30 days are escalated
Review never completed
ADR submitted for review; stakeholders do not respond within the review period; ADR is never accepted or rejected
Architecture lead has responsibility to drive the review to completion; unresponded reviews are escalated after the review period deadline
90-day review skipped
Accepted ADRs accumulate past their review date with no review; ADR index shows stale review dates
Calendar invites at acceptance time; operations team tracks overdue ADR reviews as part of monthly operational health check
Implementation diverges silently
The code has drifted from the ADR; neither the ADR nor the code is updated; the ADR appears accurate but is not
90-day review explicitly checks that the implementation matches the ADR; discrepancies are treated as high-priority findings
Superseding ADR skips explanation
New ADR replaces old one with a new decision but no explanation of what changed or what was learned
ADR template requires 'Reason for supersession' and 'What was learned' sections; architecture lead does not approve a superseding ADR without these sections
Automating the Review Cadence

Manual calendar tracking of ADR review dates fails at scale. When an integration has 12–20 ADRs, each with its own 90-day review cycle and varying out-of-cycle triggers, the overhead of tracking review dates by hand is enough to cause the system to break down. Automating the review cadence removes the administrative burden and replaces it with reliable system notifications.
Integrating with the Pull Request Workflow

The most reliable mechanism for enforcing the ADR lifecycle is integrating it with the pull request workflow. Any code change that affects an accepted ADR's scope should trigger a check that the ADR is still current. This is implemented as a CI step that identifies changed files and checks whether any related ADRs are due for review.

# .github/workflows/adr-check.yml
name: ADR Review Check
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**'
      - 'docs/adr/**'

jobs:
  check-adr-dates:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check for overdue ADR reviews
        run: python3 scripts/adr_review_checker.py
        # Fails the build if any Accepted ADR has an overdue review
        # (exit code 1 from the script)
      - name: Validate ADR index is current
        run: python3 scripts/validate_adr_index.py
        # Checks that every Accepted ADR appears in docs/adr/index.md
        # with a matching status and review date
The Lightweight ADR: When Full Process Is Disproportionate

The full ADR lifecycle — formal review, approval quorum, 90-day schedule, explicit supersession — is appropriate for high-impact, long-lived architectural decisions. For smaller integrations, early-stage PoC work, or genuinely low-stakes decisions, the overhead may be disproportionate to the benefit.
A lightweight ADR variant preserves the core practice (write it down, record the reasoning, maintain a status) with reduced process overhead:

LIGHTWEIGHT ADR: [Title]
Date: YYYY-MM-DD
Author: [name]
Status: Draft | Accepted | Superseded | Deprecated

Context:
[Two to four sentences describing the situation and why a decision is needed.]

Decision:
[One to three sentences describing what was decided.]

Rationale:
[One to three bullet points listing the primary reasons for this choice over alternatives.]

Consequences:
[Two to four bullet points listing the expected outcomes, positive and negative.]

Review: [date + 60 days — lightweight ADRs use a 60-day review]
The lightweight ADR is appropriate when:
The integration is in PoC phase and decisions are expected to change before production
The decision affects only one team and has no cross-team implications
The decision is reversible within a sprint with limited downstream impact
The integration serves fewer than 50 users and cost/quality optimisation is not yet a priority

The lightweight ADR graduates to a full ADR when the integration moves to production or when the decision's scope expands to affect multiple teams or services. The author adds the full ADR header fields (Deciders, approval record, 90-day review date) at that point — the lightweight ADR becomes the context section of the full ADR.

A lightweight ADR that is never written down is still the worst outcome. The goal of the full ADR process is institutional memory and accountability at scale; the goal of the lightweight variant is to get something written down when the alternative is nothing. Write the lightweight ADR now and upgrade it when the stakes increase.
Conducting the 90-Day Review: A Practical Walkthrough

The 90-day review is a 30-60 minute synchronous meeting between the architecture lead and operations lead, with the ADR open in front of them. The following walkthrough describes how to run an effective review for a Claude integration ADR.
Before the Meeting
Pull the current ADR from the ADR index
Gather 90 days of operational data: cost per request by tier, error rates, latency P95, fallback invocation rate, eval scores
Note any Anthropic announcements (model releases, pricing changes, capability updates) since the last review
Check whether the implementation matches the ADR: review the relevant code modules and confirm the model names, max_tokens, and timeout values match what the ADR specifies
During the Meeting
Read the decision section aloud. Ask: is this still what the system does?
Review the consequences section. Ask: did the predicted consequences materialise? Were there unexpected consequences?
Review the alternatives section. Ask: do any of the rejected alternatives look more attractive now given what we have learned?
Check the context section against current conditions. Ask: has anything in the context changed (model landscape, pricing, usage volume, user population)?
Record the outcome: re-accept, amend, or initiate supersession.
After the Meeting
Update the ADR status field with the review outcome and the next review date
If amending: update the affected sections and put the amendment through the short review cycle (3 business days)
If superseding: begin the new DRAFT ADR; keep the existing ADR active until the new one is accepted
Update the ADR index with the new review date
Close the calendar event for the completed review and schedule the next one

The 90-day review that finds nothing to change is not a wasted review — it is a confirmation that the architectural decision is holding up. Record that outcome explicitly in the ADR: 'Re-accepted 2025-04-15. No changes required. Decision remains sound.' The absence of changes is meaningful information.
Summary

The ADR lifecycle — DRAFT, IN REVIEW, ACCEPTED, SUPERSEDED, DEPRECATED, REJECTED — gives architectural decisions a defined state at every point in time. The approval process ensures that decisions with architectural significance are reviewed by the right stakeholders before becoming policy. The 90-day scheduled review keeps accepted ADRs accurate as the model landscape and integration evolve. Out-of-cycle reviews address specific triggers (model releases, incidents, price changes) that require immediate attention. The supersession and deprecation processes retire decisions cleanly while preserving the historical record.

An ADR index maintained through this lifecycle is a genuine institutional memory — a record of not just what the architecture is today, but why it is that way, what was tried and rejected, and what has changed over time. That institutional memory is the compounding return on the investment in writing ADRs in the first place.
